My only remark so far is that cascaded inference can work better at very large resolution but that is not a given. Performance is tightly tied to the scene disparity distribution that is presented to the model throughout training.

Given that the model is not trained in cascaded manner, the attention operators at the lowest resolution become "overfitted" to the disparity distribution and aspect ratios encountered during training.

A `2X` change in resolution will most likely have a low impact on the evaluation given that for the base training setting resolution (`384x512`) the non-offseted correlation layer can account for over half of the entire image. (a pixel in the lowest feature map has a receptive `32x32`, and the default `1d search` is a window of `1x9`, therefore the horizontal search can account for `288` horizontal pixels in the original image).

If the offset values would push individual pixel by a value of `1` away from their previous centre (basically obtaining a dilation factor of `2`) we would end up with a correlation product that scanned almost the entire image horizontally. Hence, the importance of the training data distribution.

In this case, going up to a resolution of `768x1024` will not produce a significant change in the networks capabilities (as most of the training sets will not have pixels that are further away than a quarter of the entire image). As such, the cascaded inference can be seen as a way of bringing back the data in the original "search" distribution when faced with extremely large resolutions.

The main crux is to understand the `wait()` method. The reason why it is a bit more complex is that we have to deal with two different resource backends: One that uses futures (placement groups) and one that doesn't (budget based).

For budget-based, we have to try starting/stopping actors at different times and potentially with our own timeout mechanism as we can't await resources becoming ready.

For placement group based, we can actually await futures - in that case, we don't need our own special logic.

I tried to make the logic very clear with the in-line comments, please let me know if there is anything left unclear.

Instead of convoluting, the test suite this way, I'd expect the docker-compose command to be a link to the executable docker compose cli plugin, and the run the whole test suite twice with each docker-compose version. This is actually so in my Arch box
```
$ file /usr/bin/docker-compose
/usr/bin/docker-compose: symbolic link to /usr/lib/docker/cli-plugins/docker-compose

In the CI job, if the docker-compose binary release is simply downloaded into the /usr/bin folder instead, all tests should simply pass out-of-the-box. Then, I'd vouch to run a parallel test job that installs the docker-compose-v1 extra requirement instead so we have some guarantees that pytest-docker runs seamlessly in either compose version, until compose-v1 is deprecated in a far(?) future.

I've never really had an issue with it, and you could always remove all .coverage files first.  Coverage combine removes all of the files that it combines together, so assume it runs then there would only be a single .coverage file leftover.  And coverage combine always ignores that file and overwrites it the next time.  You could potentially end up with a extra .coverage.* file if the nox session was stopped early or something and then it would be added in the coverage combine the next time it was run.

Also, Nox doesn't actually run in parallel, it runs them all sequentially.  I'm just used to using parallel when I setup stuff in CI.  So we could change this to just use --append and let each subsequent python version just append to the single .coverage file.  Perhaps we would need to first delete the coverage file though?

One option would be to make a separate session that simply removes all coverage files first, then triggers the test session (similar to how the test session triggers the coverage one in the current implementation).

Another option would be to just make it all explicit: remove the parallel flag and define the coverage file for each run using the COVERAGE_FILE environment variable.  This is similar to what you do in GitHub actions currently after re-reviewing it.

I don't think that `quote-plus` is necessary. The `quote` method will encode spaces to `%20` where `quote-plus` will change them to `+` (unescaped). The plus sign is encoded by both methods:

```python
>>> urllib.parse.quote("+ /", safe="")
'%2B%20%2F'
>>> urllib.parse.quote_plus("+ /")
'%2B+%2F'
```

```suggestion
    result = mmap_function(0, size + offset - aligned_offset, prot, flags, fd, aligned_offset)
```

Assume that:
* offset = 0x1001
* size/length = 10

The `alligned_offset` is 0x1000. We mmap 10 bytes so the `result` will be an area of 10 bytes***.

Later, we return a pointer to `result+offset-aligned_offset` which effectively is `result+1`. Since `result` is of 10 bytes, we will end up pointing to an area of 9 bytes, while we wanted a 10 bytes area.

And that's the reason why we need to adjust `size` argument properly.

I have based my assumptions on the example taken from `man mmap` where they call `mmap` like this (`pa_offset` is the aligned size, `offset` is size):
```c
addr = mmap(NULL, length + offset - pa_offset, PROT_READ,
                       MAP_PRIVATE, fd, pa_offset);
```

and then they use the memory as (so it is similar to us returning the adjusted pointer):
```c
write(STDOUT_FILENO, addr + offset - pa_offset, length);
```
---
*** well, `mmap` will probably return a whole memory page (by default 4 kB on most or all kernels) but we probably can't assume that.

Looks like you found a bug in the verifier :)

If you commented out line 1090, then `merkle_test_marf_key_value` will generate a proof that `k1` equals `old_v`, which is not the proof the verifier asked for.  A correct client needs to be able to determine this; the given implementation does not do this.

The bug is that the verifier should take the key and value it requested the proof for, and _additionally_ verify two more things:

* The hash of the value must be equal to the first segment proof hash.  This will (correctly) cause the verifier to fail in this example, since `hash(new_v)` is not equal to `hash(old_v)`.
* The verifier needs to check that the set of segment proofs are all prefixes of the hash of the key, and that the value it requested the proof for is at the end of the path.

> probably defined in a standard

The CA/Browser Forum Baseline Requirements to be exact. From "6.1.5 Key sizes":

> For ECDSA key pairs, the CA SHALL:
> •  Ensure that the key represents a valid point on the NIST P-256, NIST P-384 or NIST P-521 elliptic curve.

The naming of the curves is a little bit difficult though. For example, for every key size there are two curves: one with parameters associated with a Koblitz curve (denoted by the `k` in the curve name) and one with verifiably random parameters (denoted by the `r` in the curve name). As we can see from the [SEC 2: Recommended Elliptic Curve Domain Parameters (Version 2.0)](https://www.secg.org/sec2-v2.pdf) Table 2, the NIST recommended curves are the **random** curves. I.e., with a `r` in the `secp` cuve name. Unfortunately, you're not going to find the random parameter key size 256 by its "secp" name in OpenSSL. As we can read in [RFC 5480](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc5480#section-2.1.1.1), for some reason the `secp256r1` curve is referred to as `prime256v1` (also in OpenSSL and in the [Current Mozilla Security/Server Side TLS Wiki document](https://wiki.mozilla.org/index.php?title=Security/Server_Side_TLS&oldid=1229478#Intermediate_compatibility_.28recommended.29).

TL;DR: sed 's/secp256k1/secp256r1/' :wink:

```python
import modin.pandas as pd
import pandas
import numpy as np
import timeit
from asv_bench.benchmarks.utils.common import execute

# def exetuce(df):
#     pass

NROWS = 1_000_000
RAND_LOW = 0
RAND_HIGH = 10_000_000

data = {
    "col0": np.tile(
        np.concatenate([np.random.randint(RAND_LOW, RAND_HIGH, NROWS // 100 - 1), [1]]),
        100,
    ),
    "col1": np.tile(
        np.concatenate([np.random.randint(RAND_LOW, RAND_HIGH, NROWS // 100 - 1), [1]]),
        100,
    ),
}

md_df = pd.DataFrame(data)
pd_df = pandas.DataFrame(data)

# drop_duplicate: 
#   input_shape = (1_000_000, 2)
#   output_shape = (10_000, 2)

NRUNS = 1
execute(md_df.sum()) # warmup
md_res = timeit.timeit(lambda: execute(md_df.drop_duplicates()), number=NRUNS)
pd_res = timeit.timeit(lambda: pd_df.drop_duplicates(), number=NRUNS)

print(f"Cumulative time of {NRUNS} runs for modin: {md_res}s.") # ~10s.
print(f"Cumulative time of {NRUNS} runs for pandas: {pd_res}s.") # ~0.3s
```

</details>

> I'm not sure about these parameters and the fact that they're present in a public method. Using them may require a deep understanding of internal partitioning logic (e.g. dealing with empty partitions) that actually shouldn't be disclosed to the outer world.

> My concern is that it's very easy to break everything with these parameters if you don't fully understand what's happening underneath. Here's an example of how easy it is to end up with an inconsistent frame using these parameters:

```python
import modin.pandas as pd
import modin.config as cfg
cfg.NPartitions.put(4)

df = pd.DataFrame({"a": [1, 2, 3], "b": [3, 4, 5]})
qc = df._query_compiler

def make_a_series(df):
    series = df[df.columns[0]]
    res = series.to_frame()
    assert len(res.columns) == 1
    return res

mf = qc._modin_frame.apply_full_axis(
    axis=1,
    # Completely legitimate arguments from high-level perspective
    func=make_a_series,
    new_columns=["__reduced__"],
    new_column_widths=[1],
    keep_partitioning=False
)

print(mf.column_widths) # [1], when it should be: [1, 0, 0, 0]
print(mf._partitions.shape) # (1, 4)
```

That is, I think avoiding module cloning wherever possible reduces unintended consequences enough to be worth some dependence on gevent internals. There is also the possibility that we could expand the set of cloned modules in a future change after the minimum set has had some time in production to give us feedback about how it works.

We need to decide whether we want this to be a True/False only kind of parameter (where False _never_ runs flattenComponents, even when the filter is specified in the ufo.lib, and True _always_ does), or we want to have a third state `None` which means "only do flatten if the filter is specified in the ufo.lib". So the compile parameter would be used to override the lib filter.

I think it's better to have a True/False only option that ignores the lib filter; the worst it can happen is the ufo lib contains the filter already and we run it twice (but it's idempotent).

This is most likely the cause of weird `pyenv` behaviour, since pyenv evaluates BASH_ENV before/during running the initialisation scripts `pyenv init`. [PEP 394](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0394/) can cause ambiguous behaviour of the `python` command, so that in this case it is symlinked to a `python2` version before pyenv can shim the executables. I am not entirely certain why this happens.

We shouldn't use mutable values as default values. If `depths` or `num_heads` get modified, due to the way that Python works, their default values will change for all new instances of the class. Effectively the default values are shared for all instances of the class and if one instance modifies it, it affects all others. This is why we often opt for the `some_value = None` idiom in this codebase.

I recommend the following:
1. Most of the parameters that change across variants such as depths, num_heads and embed_dim (excluding stochastic_depth_prob) should be mandatory and thus have no default values.
2. I would shuffle things around to make them required and I would rearrange the order of the args to make them aligned with ViT and other implementations (for example num_classes is typically added prior the bock/norm_layer

So, in API there are also `group` and `project` properties: https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/api/features.html

Regarding removing empty values from dict, see the checks below:
```
# 1
curl --header "PRIVATE-TOKEN: <token>" --data "value=true&user=" https://mygitlab.com/api/v4/features/test 
# 404 Not Found

# 2
curl --header "PRIVATE-TOKEN:<token>" --data "value=true&user=myuser" https://mygitlab.com/api/v4/features/test 
# OK: {"name":"test","state":"conditional","gates":[{"key":"boolean","value":false},{"key":"actors","value":["User:23"]}]}

# 3
curl --header "PRIVATE-TOKEN: <token>" --data "value=true&project=&group=&feature_group=&user=" https://mygitlab.com/api/v4/features/test
# 404 Not Found

# 4
curl --header "PRIVATE-TOKEN: <token>" --data "value=true&project=&group=&feature_group=" https://mygitlab.com/api/v4/features/test
# NOT OK (not expected result): {"name":"test","state":"conditional","gates":[{"key":"boolean","value":false},{"key":"groups","value":[""]}]}

# 5
curl --header "PRIVATE-TOKEN: <token>" --data "value=true" https://mygitlab.com/api/v4/features/test
# OK (expected result, should be same as the above one): {"name":"test","state":"on","gates":[{"key":"boolean","value":true}]}
```

Thus if empty values are sent (what happens), the value is set inappropriately. Examples:
1. `gitlab.features.set(name, value)` - I would expect that such feature will be set globally, but instead now it fails with 404 Not Found, because it's unable to find an empty user. This scenario #3 above.

2. `gitlab.features.set(name, value, group=existing_group)` - it will fail with 404 because empty user is sent.

3. `gitlab.features.set(name, value, user=existing_user)` - it should set a feature for given user (if user not exist, ofc it will return 404). But instead it sets it for user, empty group (like in case #4 above).

Based on above I came with idea to clean all `None` values from dict. Cause this solves the problem. If you have any better idea how to do this, I'd be happy to rewrite it :)

Unfortunately, the very act of asking for the time stamp can trigger an overflow exception, based on the current timezone! See the [appveyor test failures](https://ci.appveyor.com/project/aio-libs/aiohttp/builds/27616968/job/4i6eq555evqijl56#L3419):

```python
ERROR    aiohttp.server:web_protocol.py:380 Error handling request
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "C:\projects\aiohttp\aiohttp\web_protocol.py", line 405, in _handle_request
    resp = await self._request_handler(request)
  File "C:\projects\aiohttp\aiohttp\web_app.py", line 330, in _handle
    resp = await handler(request)
  File "C:\projects\aiohttp\tests\test_client_functional.py", line 2044, in handler
    str(int(datetime.datetime.max.timestamp())) + "; ")
OverflowError: timestamp out of range
```

`datetime.max` Is a naive datetime value and has no timezone but a timestamp is always a value relative to 1970-01-01 midnight UTC, so Python uses the local timezone to interpret naive values when converting. In timezones west from UTC that requires adding hours to the datetime value, and so we get an overflow here.

Great question! The dataclasses have `slots` but, unfortunately, only From 3.10, which is not an option just yet for an OSS. So, we can use it only without `slots`. The slotted classes are a little bit faster at attribute lookup and take a bit less memory (minus one dict, basically). You can get a similar benefit by using `typing.NamedTuple`. However, there are a few minor differences:

1. NamedTuple is immutable. You can do the same for dataclasses by setting `frozen=True` but you cannot switch it off for a NamedTuple. It is convenient, sometimes.
2. NamedTuple doesn't allow to use `functools.cached_property`. And this is **very** convenient thing, I can't overstate how nicer and faster it makes the code.
3. NamedTuple behaves like a tuple. It generates `__iter__` and some methods like `count` which may be undesired and confusing for a public API.

Now, back to "the old approach vs dataclasses". My motivation was mostly the convenience of the development since I find the associated performance impact negligible. Dataclasses provide us with:

1. Autogeneration of `__init__`, `__repr__`, and `__eq__`.
2. Again, the ability to use `cached_property`

Now, let's talk about benchmarks. There is  the code I've tested:

```python
from dataclasses import dataclass
class A:
    __slots__ = ['a']
    def __init__(self, a):
        self.a = a
@dataclass
class B:
    a: int

a = A(1)
b = B(1)
```

And there are my results:

```python
%timeit A(1) # 152 ns ± 4.98 ns per loop
%timeit B(1) # 228 ns ± 4.13 ns per loop
%timeit a.a  # 30.8 ns ± 1.1 ns per loop
%timeit b.a  # 33.9 ns ± 1.12 ns per loop
%timeit a.a = 2  # 33.9 ns ± 3.27 ns per loop
%timeit b.a = 2  # 43.7 ns ± 3.87 ns per loop
```

As you can see, there is indeed a difference. Is it negligible? Definitely yes for the lookup operation, and we don't care about assignment one, AFAIK the message is not supposed to be changed. The instantiation, however, gives a noticeable difference, at least in my example. Is it tolerable? I don't know, actually. Depends on the philosophy of the project and where it's heading. The control about `if`/`elif` together with `raise` or `return` is handled as a group with `if`/`else` in the same situation, through the error `no-else-raise`. So disabling one disable all of them.

I think using or not `if`/`else` or `if`/`elif` when a branch interrupts the flow can be more or less relevant depending on the situation. In asymetric branches (eg. one condition is desirable, the others are not and trigger an error flow), not using `else` or `if` makes more sense. In symetric conditions, it is a little clearer to use these keywords.

A good article about this: https://dmerej.info/blog/post/else-after-return-yea-or-nay/

If I need to choose between the two rules, I would still keep `no-else-raise`. Because from my experience on this language and others, the asymetric branches occurs more often, and in that specific case, using `else` tends to create nested branches that harm readability.

I think the sequence of steps needs to be the following:
- Stabilize the checkpoint from the NewView
     - If the Node doesn't have the checkpoint in the NewView - do nothing (TBD: start a catchup here)
- Order or re-order all 3PC batches corresponding to the NewView:
    - find a corresponding PrePrepare if it's received by a Node- 
    - request PrePrepare from other nodes if it's not fiound
    - apply all PrePrepares if they are not ordered yet 
    - send all Prepares and Commits even for already ordered PrePrepares
- Unstash 3pc messages waiting for a new view
- reset the monitor
- Master Primary can start sending new PrePrepares once all PrePrepares from the old view (from NewView msg) are applied
- Backup Primaries can start sending PrePrepares once Master Instance ordered all PrePrepares from the old view (from NewView msg)

This is a sentinel type. We don't expect it to correspond to any actual `Field` in a `BUILD` file. It solely exists so that plugin authors can say `UnionRule(PythonLibraryField, TypeChecked)`.

There are other possibilities:
1. Name this something better to reflect that it's a sentinel and not a normal `Field`.
2. Instead of `UnionRule(PythonLibraryField, TypeChecked)`, have `UionRule(PythonLibrary, TypeChecked)`. That is, expect `UnionRule(Target, Field)` instead of `UnionRule(PluginField, Field)`.
   * This reduces boilerplate pretty substantially.
   * Conceptually, it doesn't make as much sense to me as `PluginField`. For example, now you'd have to mark `PythonLibrary` with `@union`. What does that mean? That suggests to me that you can substitute `PythonLibrary` with other union members, but that's not what we're actually saying.
   * Maybe we can avoid having to mark each target with `@union`..? Maybe that can get done under-the-hood? We never actually use `await Get[A](UnionBase, member)` with `PluginField`. We solely (ab)use `UnionMembership` in this constructor, so maybe it doesn't even matter if we mark `@union` anywhere?

``

The interface this object implements is defined in `zope.tal`. That interface doesn't say very much about `getValue`, but my instinct, based on the existence of `setGlobal` and `setLocal` is that `getValue` should examine the entire stack of scopes, not just the current scope. It seems to make sense to do what this was doing, just iterate from current to oldest instead of the reverse.

I think code in `zope.tal` supports this. For example, [here](https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.tal/blob/f3a8f2007361800effb4b730cad4327b78ffe43e/src/zope/tal/talinterpreter.py#L895) it appears to be wanting any current value before shadowing it in the current scope:
```python
        # We want 'macroname' name to be always available as a variable
        outer = self.engine.getValue('macroname')
        self.engine.setLocal('macroname', macroName.rsplit('/', 1)[-1])
```

I think the tests down below also basically support this interpretation, as they access shadowed variables and get values from outer scopes.

But they do that directly using `.vars`, not `getValue`. They only reason they work is because `beginScope` starts with a copy of the current `.vars`. Since `setGlobal` modifies every scope on the stack, I think this works out OK. Presumably this (directly using `.vars`, and it being a copy) was done for variable lookup speed, although it makes nested scopes more expensive.

If that makes sense, a comment explaining why there's no need to iterate would be appreciated and save having to do this analysis in the future.

Aside
====
I thought for a minute that the bug here in `getValue` may be hidden by another bug in `zope.tal`, the handling of compiled expressions that access `setGlobal` [appears to be questionable](https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.tal/blob/f3a8f2007361800effb4b730cad4327b78ffe43e/src/zope/tal/talinterpreter.py#L582-L593):
```python
    def do_setLocal(self, notused):
        pass
    def do_setLocal_tal(self, stuff):
        (name, expr) = stuff
        self.engine.setLocal(name, self.engine.evaluateValue(expr))
    bytecode_handlers["setLocal"] = do_setLocal

    def do_setGlobal_tal(self, stuff):
        (name, expr) = stuff
        self.engine.setGlobal(name, self.engine.evaluateValue(expr))
    bytecode_handlers["setGlobal"] = do_setLocal
```

but it turns out a different set of bytecode handlers [are used for tal expressions](https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.tal/blob/f3a8f2007361800effb4b730cad4327b78ffe43e/src/zope/tal/talinterpreter.py#L1007-L1008):
```python
    bytecode_handlers_tal["setLocal"] = do_setLocal_tal
    bytecode_handlers_tal["setGlobal"] = do_setGlobal_tal
```

So ``Werror`` is useful to prevent warnings slipping in to the openthread codebase. This is important for:
1. Contributors to the openthread repo who want their code merged
2. The CI tool to verify
Any other users of the openthread repo will find it to be an annoyance, as anything from upgrading compiler to changing the target can generate warnings that aren't necessarily important. I know openthread extensively checks different compilers in CI, but it still isn't impossible. Also if a contributor wants to try something out in a quick-hack kind of way before dedicating the time to a proper implementation, then warnings can happen and just get in the way.

So really I think it is better to have an opt-in method of ``Werror`` like we have now, where the onus is on the CI tool to be configured correctly, and the contributors to use ccmake/cmake-gui/``-DOT_COMPILE_WARNING_AS_ERROR=ON`` to enable it.

```python
def get_next_query_string(request: HttpRequest) -> str | None:
    """
    Get the query string (including the prefix `?`) to redirect to after a successful POST.
    
    If a query string can't be determined, returns None.
    """
    # If the view function smells like a class-based view, we can interrogate it.
    try:
        view = request.resolver_match.func.view_class()
        redirect_field_name = view.redirect_field_name
    except AttributeError:
        # Interrogation failed :(
        return None

    view.request = request
    query_params = request.GET.copy()
    success_url = view.get_success_url()
    if success_url:
        query_params[redirect_field_name] = success_url
    if query_params:
        return f"?{urlencode(query_params)}"
    return None
```

Since `scheme` is a keyword parameter, a non-keyword parameter cannot come after it in the parameter list. I added `"utf-8"` so as not reorder the parameters and break backwards compatibility.

Instead, I can import `defaults` and use `encoding` defined there:

```python
from commitizen.defaults import encoding
...

def get_metadata(
    filepath: str, scheme: VersionScheme = Pep440, encoding: str = encoding
) -> dict:
    ...
```

Python still [defaults to the 8-bit 1252 character set encoding on Windows](https://docs.python.org/3/using/windows.html#utf-8-mode). Making `"utf-8"` the default encoding is a good choice and shouldn't be problematic (see `Code that may break` in [PEP 528](https://peps.python.org/pep-0528/) and [PEP 529](https://peps.python.org/pep-0529/) for impetus).

Files opened in binary mode [return contents as `bytes` objects without any decoding](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#open), so `encoding` wasn't added to those uses of `open` (nor for trivial cases like `commands/init.py/is_python_poetry()`.

I'll add `.encoding` everywhere else where it was missed.

I don't think using the `netgo` build constraint is required. Go can be configured to use system DNS resolution, or use a go DNS resolver internally. If cgo isn't available, and there is no DNS resolution available, the internal resolver will be used instead.

Looking at the source, my bet is that if `cgo` isn't available, `preferGo()` will return `true` if `netGo` hasn't been explicitly set.

https://github.com/golang/go/blob/go1.13.7/src/net/conf.go#L130-L132

In any case, `CGO_ENABLED` is the big hammer that disables `cgo`, so if the `net` package required `cgo`, the build would fail.

There is some additional context in https://go-review.googlesource.com/c/go/+/11584/.

Because Horovod synchronizes on the allreduce, we can feel confident that if we call commit at the end of the batch, we should not need to worry about workers being out of sync.  So it should not be possible for some workers to get ahead of others.  If a worker fails in between allreduce and commit, then we should still be okay, because the gradient updates of the failing worker should still be applied to the other non-failing workers.

Assuming neither of the `DONT_USE` variables are set:


**On master:**
If `PYENV_ROOT` is set, but `pyenv` the binary is not on `PATH`, the stacktrace posted in #4245 occurs.

**In this PR:**
If `PYENV_ROOT` is set, but `pyenv` the binary is not on `PATH`, the following logic is used:
- If a `pyenv` executable can be located in one of three locations, it will be used.
- If not, Pipenv will search for an `asdf` binary using the equivalent search process.

This provides two benefits:
1. `pyenv`/`asdf` is searched for more aggressively and don't need to be on `PATH`.
2. Fallback from `pyenv` to `asdf` will occur more reliably.

Oh you are bringing up an interesting case there! When we set `A2` as a new checkpoint it becomes the new canonical head and we stop caring about `A3`. I think that is the way it should be (For some reason, the user decided to set the checkpoint to `A2`, imho that should be given precedence).

But know what happens is that `A2` is closing a gap "backwards" which isn't what we want here! We get into a situation where our gap tracking would think we have a well-connected chain which isn't the case!

So, my idea is that `persist_checkpoint_header` must be altered to notice situations when it is backwards filling a gap and then do one of these things:

1. Raise an exception. Let client code now that they can not pick this checkpoint before they bring the database into a different state

2. Alternatively, remove the `B1` header to create a new gap to continue to track this missing connection. Removing just the single header before `A2` should be enough because we do not know where exactly the fork might be (which are uncles, which are actual parents)

Okay, in my mind, there's two things that could happen in the event of _following_ a back-point (which I think is the only interaction with backpointers that matters while generating a segment proof -- if it's just a sibling node to the path being proved, you just simply include it's pruned hash). It's not clear which of those is being described:

1. When you follow a back-pointer, you treat the back-pointer like any other node in the Merkle path -- basically you hash the contents of the linked node and continue processing.
2. When you follow a back-pointer to block _m_, you create a new Merkle path from the root of _m_ to the intermediate node you followed.

Reading back over the description of using the _block hash_ for back pointer's hashes, I think the answer is (2). Is this is also why shunt proofs are required as described: you need to prove the inclusion (and block height) of each block used in the segment proofs?

As a further test of my understanding, the reason for doing this is to avoid copying the node hash in addition to the blockhash (which is necessary for lookups in the fork table in any case)?

Hmm, it sounds like this issue happens only when using a single `Writer` from multiple processes at once, which this doesn't work because `fork()` is broken for multi-threaded code (see e.g. https://thorstenball.com/blog/2014/10/13/why-threads-cant-fork/).

I don't think we really want to support that use case.  If somebody is using multiprocessing to parallelize training, they should use a separate Writer instance in each process.  Otherwise they will have far more problems than just deadlocks as described in the issue - for example, the same Writer across multiple forked processes is presumably using the same file descriptor, but writes to it aren't synchronized (because locks don't have any effect for multiprocess code) so it would be possible to end up with conflicting or overlapping writes to the same file, resulting in data corruption.

I posted this in another comment, but we should have a way to disable Aladdin calls for a period of time if it encounters repeated errors. The scenarios I'm thinking about are:

1. A user in an air-gapped cloud will never be able to hit the service (we are working on a way to download the model and query it locally, but that won't involve the service itself), so they should not waste cycles trying to hit an endpoint that does not exist for them.
2. A user experiencing a temporary internet outage will want this to work once they reconnect, so any disabling should have a time limit to prevent the feature from being disabled forever.

This code show that we can save about 1.5 seconds for a very long ragged array (25 mio)

`lengths` is an 25 mio long array of lengths
`trainIDS_` is an 25 mio long array of arrays of tokens

def l1(lengths): 
...totalToks=0
...for l in lengths: totalToks += l
...return totalToks
def l2(items): 
...totalToks=0
...for rag in items: totalToks += len(rag)
...return totalToks    
print(f"number of rags:{len(trainIDS_)}")
%timeit l1(lengths)
%timeit l2(trainIDS_)

The result is:
> number of rags:24871686
> 2.45 s ± 55.5 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each)
> 3.94 s ± 27.9 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each)

```
>>> df = pd.DataFrame({
...        "movie": ["spam", "ham"],
...        "genre": [["action"], ["comedy", "romance"]]
... })
>>> type(df["genre"][0])
<class 'list'>
>>> df
  movie              genre
0  spam           [action]
1   ham  [comedy, romance]

```
>>> df = pa.table(df).to_pandas()
>>> type(df["genre"][0])
<class 'numpy.ndarray'>
>>> df
  movie              genre
0  spam           [action]

There's no need for constructors or init methods in our smart contracts.  You can just add code at the very end that will be executed when the contract is instantiated (since to the VM, it's all the same).

Example:

```
 (define-map pythags ((a int) (b int) (c int)) ((result bool)))

(define-public (test-pythag-triple (a int) (b int) (c int))
   (let
     (
         (a2 (* a a))
         (b2 (* b b))
         (c2 (* c c))
     )
     (begin
       (if (eq? (+ a2 b2) c2)
           (set-entry! pythags (tuple (a a) (b b) (c c)) (tuple (result 'true)))
           (set-entry! pythags (tuple (a a) (b b) (c c)) (tuple (result 'false)))
       )
       'true
     )
   )
)

(define-public (is-pythag-triple (a int) (b int) (c int))
    (let 
      (
        (result (get result (fetch-entry pythags (tuple (a a) (b b) (c c)))))
      )
      (begin
        (if (eq? result 'null)
           'false
           result
        )
      )
   )
)

(begin
  (test-pythag-triple 3 4 5)
  (test-pythag-triple 1 2 3)
  'null
)
```

Here, `test-pythag-triple` will be called twice with `3 4 5` and `1 2 3` when the contract is instantiated.

One of the things I like about bumpversion is it removes the need to calculate version numbers, instead you pass `"major"`, `"minor"`, "`patch"`, "`release`", and it _does the right thing_.

It took me a while to figure out but I think the current config is correct:
1. `bumpversion major`: this indicates the next release will be a major release, version is `MAJOR.0.0.dev`
2. `bumpversion release`: Releases the current version (enable tagging), version is `MAJOR.0.0`
3. `bumpversion patch`: this indicates the next release will be a patch release, version is `MAJOR.0.1.patch`
4. `bumpversion minor`: the next release will be a minor release instead of a patch, version is `MAJOR.1.0.patch`
5. `bumpversion release`: Releases the current version (enable tagging), version is `MAJOR.1.0`

Not sure how standard it is, I think I first found in on cppreference (https://en.cppreference.com/w/cpp/types/numeric_limits/epsilon).

`EPSILON` is the distance between `1.0` and the next largest value that can be represented, we then scale it by a value that's on the same order of magnitude as the values (I've seen variations here where there is no scaling done, or the scaling is done by multiplying by the larger of the two values). The idea is that the scaled epsilon value is now about the same size as large as the smallest change in value at the same order of magnitude as the values. We then scale it again by some fudge factor (5 in this case). The final comparison is between the difference between the given values and this fudged smallest representable value.

This isn't always a good way to compare floating point values, but in this case we're trying to verify that the value has been decoded properly from the binary stream (and we aren't performing any mathematical operation on them). I think this comparison against the unit of least precision (ULP) makes sense.

I would have love to but when coming back to this issue I finally understood what was happening here..

As I showed in the updated examples, I am using this code structure with aioredlock:
```py
try:
  lock = await lock_manager.lock()
except LockError as e:
  ... log error or raise
else:
  ... if no exception, run the code protected by the lock
  await lock_manager.unlock(lock)
finally:
  await lock_manager.destroy()
```
In this case, the `finally` block will be executed whether or not there was an error when acquiring the lock.
But if we get an exception in `set_lock` then the code in `algorithm.py` will catch it and schedule a task to cleanup later:
https://github.com/joanvila/aioredlock/blob/49dc1aff64c50bc59c17735d09a2922e03171f1c/aioredlock/algorithm.py#L96-L101
It conflicts with the `destroy` we do ourselves which itself calls `unset_lock` and destroys our `Instance` objects.
When the scheduled task is executed, there is no instances to clean anymore as they were destroyed and with the conditions in `unset_lock` we end up here:
https://github.com/joanvila/aioredlock/blob/49dc1aff64c50bc59c17735d09a2922e03171f1c/aioredlock/redis.py#L403

That's why I had to protect the `raise LockError(...) from [None][0]` in my previous comment.
When not using `finally` in my code then no problem anymore and I can remove the default value to raise from.

Unfortunately this reveals an issue with the current Aioredlock code that need to be fixed in another PR: if there is an exception on one of the instance when setting a lock then resources acquired on other instances are never cleaned up. Not cool.

In the Go agent I make the Flush method block until the server has sent the queued events to the server, successfully or not. I did this primarily to make testing simpler and more robust. The way I did it is roughly this:

- when `flush` is called, check if the transport has a `flushed` event attribute already. If not, set a new one, and then send the flush request to the queue and wait on the event to be signalled
- when the server has finished processing the flush request, signal the event and clear the `flushed` event field

You might want to do this eventually, otherwise you'll probably run into intermittent failures on slow CI machines due to the sleeps.

This `dispatch_uid` is unique per dependency, not per a pair of dependent + dependency. Consider this code:
```python
class Tag(Model):
    name = CharField(...)

class User(Model):
    random_tags = ManyToManyField(Tag)

class Cat(Model):
    color_tags = ManyToManyField(Tag)

auditlog.register(User, m2m_fields={"random_tags": []})
auditlog.register(Cat, m2m_fields={"color_tags": []})
```
In this case we won't track the changes to `Cat.color_tags`, because its handler will have the same `dispatch_uid` as the one for `User.random_tags` (please correct me if I misunderstand the logic of `dispatch_uid`).

A better way would be to generate `dispatch_uid` from a pair of `User, Tag` and `Cat, Tag` respectively.

Well, the formula that is computed here is basically
```
xo[j] = (x == NA)? 0 : 
          ASC? (x - min + 1) :
               (max - x + 1);
```
That "+1" makes it so that `x==min` maps into the value 1, which is right after the map value of NA (which is 0). But with the alternative placement of NAs, you'd have to modify this formula so that it says
```
xo[j] = (x == NA)? (max - min + 1) : 
          ASC? (x - min) :
               (max - x);
```
So you can either make a bigger nested if-condition, or maybe try to combine both cases into one. For example, write `(x - min + NAFIRST)` where `NAFIRST=(na_pos=='first')?1:0`.

The wording here is a bit obtuse, and was intended to leave the door open for nested unknowns--something that we use in practice, but something that isn't directly addressed in the doc and that I was hoping to leave out of the abstract model. Nested unknowns--i.e. unknown values inside of otherwise known object/map/array outputs--arise pretty frequently when dealing with previews, where they may be produced by resource providers as part of previewing a create or update operation.

Given the definitions in the current doc, the value inside a single output is either fully known or fully unknown. Given those semantics, the only way to build a partially-knowable value is to use nested outputs. This is essentially the mirror of `inputShape`, but for outputs, so let's look at the `Array<string>` example from the output perspective: if we want to produce an `Output<Array<string>>` where each element may be known or unknown, we need to use a type like `Output<Union<Array<string>, Array<Output<string>>>>`, or simply `Output<Array<Output<string>>>`. Projecting these types is pretty raw and unwieldy, as they can requires a great deal of unwrapping as nesting depth increases.

Instead of projecting these types, the various SDKs tend to opt to project the simpler `Output<Array<string>>` type, and use an internal representation for the concrete value that includes distinguished unknown values. This approach allows the SDKs to allow e.g. lifted property and element access into partially-known composite values. For example, the Node SDK will allow the user to access an element of an `Output<[]string>` via a proxied index operator even if some elements of the array are unknown, though it will _not_ allow the user to access the entire value via `apply`.

So to answer your original question: at the model level, "may produce an unknown" should be "must produce an unknown". I'll change the wording and work on adding a brief section regarding nested `Ouptut<T>` values after the combinators.

>Is it better to have one of our Pubsub processing apps pause while it deals with this sort of spike in its backlog, or keep going at the potential expense of causing a bunch of upcoming tasks to retry?
But it won't pause immediately, will it? Acker will stop yes, but all other tasks will still have to compete with those 2500 requests until all internal queues are full and back pressure make it all the way from acker to producers.
What is the worst case scenario? Assuming there's no free CPU resources.
1. (Await) Acker is blocked. In the meantime subscriber continues to work until all internal queues are filled. Then it sends 2500 requests, waits until the last one receives a response and then resumes work as usual.
2. (Fire and forget) Our really mean scheduler puts acker on halt, then lets internal queues to fill, then schedules 2500 requests, but then it has to give acker a turn.

We're really choosing between degrading latency for unknown random amount for `time to schedule 2500 requests + time to schedule receiving their responses` time window or add up to `time to schedule 2500 requests + receive response for the longest of them` of latency to unknown number of messages in a range from `[number of messages currently in the system]` to `[max number of messages in the system]`.

Thanks for the review!

> Is this going to be added to `.gitattributes`?

I considered marking it as binary in `.gitattributes`. I like it as is
because it’s human-readable, it only reflects our direct deps (not the
whole lockfile) and so won’t get too much churn, and its diffs are
human-readable and the blame on it will be useful. I’ll merge as is just
for expediency, but I’d be happy to change course if you’d like that or
if it becomes a problem later on.

> Also, which command generated this file?

This is generated with the workflow described in `server/DEVELOPMENT.md`
under the “Adding third-party dependencies” section. The specific
command that I always pull from my shell history is

```
(tb && rm -rf third_party/rust/ && cd tensorboard/data/server && cargo fetch && cargo raze)
```

(where `tb` is a `cd` to the repository root), which is basically an
abbreviation of the steps in that doc.

The file is new because `cargo-raze` now supports “Cargo workspaces”,
which are a way to have multiple packages in one repo. (Think of it kind
of like Lerna in the NPM world.) This means that the global namespace of
`//third_party/rust` is replaced with a per-`Cargo.toml` namespace. We
only have one `Cargo.toml`, so it’s just a move for us—but now that we
have workspace support I might actually want to take advantage of it to
separate out the gRPC code from the core.

> doesn't always do what you're thinking, and doesn't work in all operating systems

Oh interesting! I went researching for what you meant by this statement, and see the following in `?parallel::detectCores()`

```text
logical | if possible, use the number of physical CPUs/cores (if FALSE) or logical CPUs (if TRUE).
Currently this is honoured only on macOS, Solaris and Windows.
```

II also noticed this:

```text
An integer, NA if the answer is unknown.
```

Now that I see that it's possible for this function to return `NA`, I think that for maximum portability the default value should be set to `0L` (meaning the value of `omp_get_num_threads()`), instead of using any of R's mechanisms for detecting the available CPUs. That way, we wouldn't need to worry about an `NA` being passed through to that parameter (I'm not sure how that would be handled on the C++ side).

Setting the default to `0L` would also be consistent with the default on the C++ side:

* https://lightgbm.readthedocs.io/en/latest/Parameters.html#num_threads
* https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/60e72d5f4ec5ec2a4d30a53633e1e2d59c81309b/include/LightGBM/config.h#L205

And technically consistent with the default in the `scikit-learn` estimators in the Python package, where a value of `-1` is passed, since any value `<= 0` is treated by LightGBM as "take the result of `omp_get_num_threads()`".

* https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/60e72d5f4ec5ec2a4d30a53633e1e2d59c81309b/python-package/lightgbm/sklearn.py#L365
* https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/60e72d5f4ec5ec2a4d30a53633e1e2d59c81309b/include/LightGBM/utils/openmp_wrapper.h#L30-L34
*

@sallysyw I think we can do better. There are multiple pros in converging towards a generalizable recipe on which we can build upon rather than having custom recipes per model, especially if those custom recipes achieve lower accuracies as it is in this case. 

TorchVision's new recipe (published [here](https://pytorch.org/blog/how-to-train-state-of-the-art-models-using-torchvision-latest-primitives/)), when combined with some of the ViT specific optimizations you introduced (such as gradient clipping and the RA sampler) and some proposals from the [community](https://github.com/pytorch/vision/pull/5084#issuecomment-998369197), achieves much better results than the ones included in this PR. 

Here are some quick runs I did to confirm that the new recipe works well on ViT:
```
vit_b_16 (jobid 11379, epoch 450)
Acc@1 80.730 Acc@5 95.228

vit_l_32 (jobid 12096, epoch 378)
Acc@1 75.050 Acc@5 91.824

vit_l_16 (jobid 12107/12349/12567, epoch 378)
Acc@1 79.668 Acc@5 94.660
```

Given the above have 0.7-0.8 point higher accuracy, we should prefer them. Of course you can further optimize them by tuning the hyper-parameters but we should at least deploy the best available models. I think the condition here `index < kMaxLength` is correct (in the sense that we want `index < 255` to be checked here and if `index` gets to `255` then the name should be considered as invalid).

My understanding is that the entire encoded name should be `255` bytes of less. Note that the name should always end with a "terminator" or a zero length byte. Here in `AppendMultipleLabels()` we should not go to 255 to leave room for the "terminator" zero byte (which is added by `AppendName()`).

That said, it may be good to clarify the definition of `kMaxLength` itself and what it represents.
- If we consider a string representation of a DNS name (i.e. a string of dot `'.'` seperated sequence of labels), then the string representation's length can be at most `254` chars (or `253` char if the string omits the the ending `'.'` , i.e., using `example.com` instead o`example.com.` ).
   -  I am using "string length" definition here that does not include the `'\0'` null char at the end of string which is the commonly used notion.
   - So if the string representation has a length of `255` then it is invalid.
- Here is reason for `254` length:
  - The encoded name format is sequence of `"len (byte)"` followed by `len` chars ending with `"zero len (byte)"` and is limited to 255 bytes total.
  - When we represent it as string we use dot `'.'` for all `"len (byte)"` except for the very first one, so we end up with `254` chars as max length.
  - Note that this assumes there would no escaping of chars in the name (which is possible/recommended in case of "service instance name" (which can contain more characters than other labels).

Here is my suggestion:
- We define a private `enum` value of `kMaxEncodedSize = 255` to represent max encoded size and and use in the `AppendMultipleLabels()` to make this clear.
- We can also clarify/update the definition of `Name::kMaxLength` that it represent the maximum length of string representation of a DNS name 
   - I actually have such change  in the DNS-SD branch that I am working on. In that we define `kMaxSize = 255` (size instead of length) to indicate the "size" of char array to pass to read a name (which is again `255` to include the null char at the end of string).
  

(see related discussion here [stackoverflow.com/what-is-the-maximum-length-of-a-dns-name](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/32290167/what-is-the-maximum-length-of-a-dns-name)).

This is correct (and the logic here wasn't touched in this PR):

1) ray.put places all the to-be-synched new weights in the object store and returns a object ref.
2) This object ref is used in the call to `set_weights.remote(...)`.
Note that if we had called `set_weights.remote()` with the actual weights (numpy), ray would have automatically transferred these to the object store first (and would have had to do so for all n remote workers, which is inefficient).

```suggestion
    def _peek(self) -> Dataset[T]:
        if self._first_dataset is not None:
            self._optimize_stages()
            self._dataset_iter = PipelineExecutor(self)
            self._first_dataset = next(self._dataset_iter)
        return self._first_dataset

I find a condition like `keepbits >= 0` reasonable, but note that `keepbits < 0` within reasonable limits (see below) shouldn't cause problems as this will just round the exponent bits. E.g.
```julia
julia> using BitInformation
julia> round(4f0,-1)
2.0f0
```
So in this format only ...,0.125,0.5,2,8,... are representable. However, as the exponent bits describe logarithmically distributed floats, round to nearest is now round to nearest in log-space. Meaning that while 4 is round down to 2 in the example above,
```julia
julia> round(4.1f0,-1)
8.0f0
```
although in lin-space 4.1 is closer to 2 than to 8. Sure, this has to be treated with caution, as NaN/Inf are defined through their exponents, meaning you end up with situations like
```julia
julia> round(NaN32,-1)
-0.0f0
julia> round(Inf32,-1)
-0.0f0
```
And the carry bit can propagate into the sign bit (for |x|>2), which is also weird
```julia
julia> round(4f0,-8)
-0.0f0
julia> round(-4f0,-8)
0.0f0
```

Hmmm, @Zeroto521, on second thought, I think we need a bit better definition of the semantics. `min_max_scale` currently operates with the assumption of operating on one column, so the use of `column_name` makes sense here. Below is my attempt at reasoning through the multiple ways we could use `min_max_scale`.

- Scale one column.
- Scale multiple columns independently.
- Scale multiple columns, but jointly (so they are all scaled to the same min and max)

`entire_data` is a special case of scaling multiple columns _jointly_. 

Since we're working on this function, it's a good chance to change the API to be flexible yet also sensible. What if the API was, instead the following?

```python
def min_max_scale(df, feature_range, column_names: Iterable[Hashable] | Callable, jointly: bool):
```

If `jointly` is True, then the `column_names` provided are jointly scaled; otherwise, they are not. 

I wanted to point out a new behaviour that we might be able to support across the rest of the API -- by making `column_names` accept a Callable that has the signature:

```python
def column_names_callable(df) -> Iterable[Hashable]:
```

we can enable `min_max_scale` on all columns by doing:

```python
df.min_max_scale(feature_range=(0, 1), column_names = lambda df: df.columns, jointly=True)
```

This is pretty concise without resorting to needing to maintain string mappings for special-case behaviour.

I'm glad we didn't rush to merge this PR, giving us the time and space to think clearly about the semantics of the API.

For the functionality, I think our code can work even there are empty partitions. I checked SparkXShards operations, only partition_by() and to_ray() use rdd.mapPartitions(). Others use rdd.map(). rdd.map() can work even there are empty partitions. In partition_by() operation, it first calls rdd.partitionBy(), which rearranges the elements almost evenly to partitions so the empty partitions would be removed, then calls rdd.mapPartitions(). For to_ray() operation, I think we may often call xshards.repartition() before to_ray(), repartition would  re-dispatch elements too, so to_ray() may not store empty partition data to plasma. I think I understand your question now. We keep the dependency for each ActorHandle so that we know which tasks have executed so far and which task from each handle can execute next. The former is mostly important for failure scenarios (e.g., making sure you don't re-execute the same task twice on an actor), and the latter comes up during normal execution when there are multiple tasks from the same handle that could execute.

Both of these things don't strictly need the "dependency"; you could accomplish the same thing with task counters for each handle. However, it's convenient to use dependencies since this is also how we determine whether non-actor tasks can be executed.

Context:

- We’ve got rid of the @wandb_mixin decorator in favor of a setup_wandb(config) function for W&B
- We’re doing the same for MlFlow
- The question is, should we use a config-API setup_mlflow(config) or  and explicit API setup_mlflow(experiment_name, experiment_id, tracking_uri, …) 

Pro config:
- Same way we used to configure the mixins
- Required for W&B so it logs the config to the run (otherwise users have to do this manually. They are used to passing it to wandb.init() )
- We should be consistent between wandb and mlflow mixins

Pro args:
- Explicit about which args are supported
- Passing the mixin configs via config was a workaround because this was the easiest way to pass these settings and still retain some flexibility. But with the explicit setup_xyz() APIs this workaround isn’t needed anymore

Proposal:
- How about we go with setup_mlflow(config, experiment_name, experiment_id) (similar for wandb). If a mlflow or - wandb config key is set, we print a deprecation warnings and tell people to pass the configs as proper arguments instead.
- This will get rid of the “special” config keys but we can still log the rest of the config automatically.

It's been bugging me why this wouldn't be picked up by the type checker. I think I've tracked it down to the fact that `LoadJob`, `QueryJob`, etc all derive from `_AsyncJob`, which in turn derives from `google.api_core.future.polling.PollingFuture`. The problem is that `google.api_core.future.polling.PollingFuture` is not typable, so it gets turns into an `Any` type, which makes all the job types equivalent and therefore doesn't generate an error. When testing with mypy you have to add `# type: ignore` to the `PollingFuture` import line explicitly, but I guess `pytype` is more forgiving.

I've create the attached file demonstrating the problem (annoyingly github won't let me attach the file as a .py). As currently written it'll generate an error in both mypy and pytype, but swap the comments on lines 5 and 6 and the error goes away.

Anyway, I have a reasonable workaround, so if you want to leave this that's absolutely fine. If in future the `python-api-core` library adds typing then I expect this to break though. Adding an `isinstance(query_job, job.QueryJob` will resolve the issue.

Cheers,
Andrew

I made every attempt to keep things exactly as they were here, but as `__version__` is not a static attribute and requires dynamic runtime code execution, `setuptools` fails to statically retrieve it from the AST during installation without importing the package and executing arbitrary code, which in turns triggers the complex, environment-dependent logic in `__init__.py` (which we _definitely_ don't want running on install) and crashes due to missing third party imports which haven't been installed yet. Furthermore, I tested defining a static `__version__` in `_version.py` and keeping everything else as it is, but it still triggers executing the `__init__` as before, and thus the same crash on install.

So, the simplest solution for this PR that didn't require risky hacks or much bigger changes (e.g. using `setuptools_scm`, a VERSION file, or another third-party tool) was to simply include `__version__` in `__init__.py` directly, particularly given the motivation of a separate `_version.py` to begin with was to allow the (now-legacy) setup.py to load and execute it, which is nowadays unnecessary and deprecated, and is exactly the behavior this PR migrates away from. See pypa/setuptools#1724 for more details (there were a few other workarounds there, but they require `__init__.py` to not have complex logic or third-party package imports, or rely on implementation-dependent hacks, and in any case didn't work in my testing).

Here's an example scenario that would result in runs for different asset selections within the same the same backfill iteration:
- Asset A -> Asset B
- Asset A -> Asset C
- A, B, and C all have different `PartitionsDefinition`s
- Partition "foo_b" of B depends on partition "foo_a" of A
- Partition "foo_c" of C depends on partition "foo_a" of A
- The user launches backfill that covers "foo_a" and all its descendant asset partitions
- In iteration 1, the backfill daemon launches a run for "foo_a" with asset selection [A]
- The run for "foo_a" of A completes
- In iteration 2, the backfill daemon launches two runs
  - "foo_b" with asset selection [B]
  - "foo_c" with asset selection [C]

# replace-stdout-stderr (UP022)

Derived from the **pyupgrade** linter.

Autofix is always available.

## What it does
Checks for uses of `subprocess.run` that send `stdout` and `stderr` to a
pipe.

## Why is this bad?
As of Python 3.7, `subprocess.run` has a `capture_output` keyword argument
that can be set to `True` to capture `stdout` and `stderr` outputs. This is
equivalent to setting `stdout` and `stderr` to `subprocess.PIPE`, but is
more explicit and readable.

## Example
```python
import subprocess

subprocess.run(["foo"], stdout=subprocess.PIPE, stderr=subprocess.PIPE)
```

Use instead:
```python
import subprocess

subprocess.run(["foo"], capture_output=True)
```

## References
- [Python 3.7 release notes](https://docs.python.org/3/whatsnew/3.7.html#subprocess)
- [Python documentation: `subprocess.run`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/subprocess.html#subprocess.run)

It's already there--credentials have their own caches. Those are more expensive because they support more complex scenarios, e.g. arbitrary scopes. Scopes are baked into this policy however, so its arguments to `get_token` will never change, and it now knows how long tokens are valid. OK, last argument on my side. If you do not agree after it, I revert to atexit.

It is true that there is no actual use in certbot codebase, server teardown will work correctly with atexit. Two things pushed me to make this change. First one is, as I said before, potential uses of this logic in other contexts in certbot.

Second one is that the reason we did this PR, so to offer a way to have an alternative to the bash scripts that will be removed, made me realized that Certbot integration tests could be run by other projects. I would expect for instance that other ACME server projects are using some tests against certbot, and maybe reuse parts of our own tests. 

So in the scope of external usages, I do not like a lot the atexit approach, as we do not offer a way to control when the teardown occurs: it is at the end of the python process, independantly from what is happening during the script itself. I already talked about pytest programmatical call on that matter.

Here I give a way to control this teardown. Future useful API could be also to provide a context manager, even if with these modifications, it is really easy to do it by yourself. 

And finally, the modifications done to implement that are very lightweight, I think it does not add some complexity. So the low usability added on our sole interest comes with a low price, it stays balanced. 

What do you think of that?
